extra.message:
Hard-coded Credentials Detected (password)

"password" detected in source code, review this potentially hard-coded credential. Hard-coding credentials in source code or binaries makes it easy for attackers to extract sensitive information.

Impact: Credential exposure, unauthorized access to systems.

Fix: Store credentials in environment variables or a secure vault, never in source code.

Insecure Deserialization via pickle.loads()

User-controlled data is passed directly to pickle.loads() without validation. Pickle deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary code execution.

Impact: Remote code execution — an attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands on the server.

Fix: Never deserialize untrusted data with pickle. Use a safe format like JSON.

Path Traversal / Arbitrary File Read (path parameter)

The path parameter is passed directly to open(os.path.abspath(...)) without validation. An attacker can read arbitrary files on the filesystem using path traversal sequences.

Impact: Arbitrary file read, source code disclosure, credential theft.

Fix: Validate the resolved path against a whitelist of allowed directories.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via path parameter

The path parameter is used in urllib.request.urlopen() without validation, allowing an attacker to make the server send requests to arbitrary URLs.

Impact: Access to internal services, port scanning, data exfiltration from internal network.

Fix: Validate the URL against a whitelist of allowed domains. Reject URLs pointing to internal/private IP ranges.

Command Injection via shell=True (domain parameter)

The domain parameter is directly concatenated into a shell command: subprocess.run("nslookup " + params["domain"], shell=True, ...). An attacker can inject shell metacharacters to execute arbitrary OS commands.

Impact: Full remote code execution on the server.

Fix: Pass the command as a list without shell=True: subprocess.run(["nslookup", params["domain"]], ...).

XML External Entity (XXE) Injection via lxml

XML parsing uses XMLParser(load_dtd=True, resolve_entities=True, no_network=False), which explicitly enables DTD loading, entity resolution, and network access, allowing XXE attacks.

Impact: Arbitrary file read, SSRF, potential denial of service via billion laughs attack.

Fix: Use lxml.etree.XMLParser(resolve_entities=False, no_network=True, load_dtd=False) or use defusedxml.

XPath Injection via Unsanitized name Parameter

The name parameter is directly interpolated into an XPath query: xpath(".//user[name/text()='%s']" % params["name"]). An attacker can inject XPath expressions to extract arbitrary data.

Impact: Extraction of sensitive data from the XML document (e.g., passwords), authentication bypass.

Fix: Use parameterized XPath queries or validate that input contains no special XPath characters.

Unchecked Input for Loop Condition (size parameter)

Loop bounds are set directly from user-controlled data via the size parameter in range(int(params["size"])). An attacker can provide extremely large values to cause denial of service.

Impact: Denial of service through excessive CPU and memory consumption.

Fix: Validate and cap the size parameter to a reasonable maximum value before using it in loop bounds.

Memory Allocation with Excessive Size Value (size parameter)

Memory allocation size is controlled by user input via the size parameter. An attacker can supply a very large value to exhaust server memory.

Impact: Denial of service through memory exhaustion.

Fix: Validate and cap the size parameter to a reasonable maximum before allocating memory.

Path Traversal via include parameter

The include parameter is passed directly to open() without validation, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files from the filesystem.

Impact: Arbitrary file read, source code disclosure, credential theft.

Fix: Validate the resolved path against a whitelist of allowed directories.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via include parameter

The include parameter is used in urllib.request.urlopen() without validation, allowing an attacker to make the server send requests to arbitrary URLs.

Impact: Access to internal services, port scanning, data exfiltration from internal network.

Fix: Validate the URL against a whitelist of allowed domains.

Dynamic Code Execution via exec() on user-controlled data

The include parameter fetches a file (local or remote) and its contents are executed with exec(program, envs). An attacker can supply a URL to a malicious script which will be executed on the server.

Impact: Full remote code execution — attacker can run arbitrary code on the server.

Fix: Remove this functionality entirely. Never execute dynamically loaded code from untrusted sources.

HTTP Response Splitting / Header Injection via charset Parameter

The charset parameter is directly embedded into the Content-Type response header without sanitization. An attacker can inject CRLF sequences to inject arbitrary HTTP headers or split the response.

Impact: HTTP response splitting, cache poisoning, XSS via injected headers, phishing.

Fix: Validate the charset parameter against a strict allowlist of known charset names. Strip or reject CRLF characters.

Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via unsanitized user-controlled data

User-controlled data is reflected in the HTTP response without proper sanitization or encoding. Multiple parameters (v, comment, redir, etc.) are embedded directly into HTML output.

Impact: Session hijacking, credential theft, malicious redirects, defacement.

Fix: HTML-encode all user-controlled data before inserting it into the response.

Stack Trace Disclosure to Client

The full Python traceback is returned to the client via traceback.format_exc() in the exception handler. This exposes internal file paths, code structure, and potentially sensitive data.

Impact: Information disclosure: internal file paths, code structure, library versions exposed to attackers.

Fix: Log the full traceback server-side only. Return a generic error message to the client.
